coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesized fluorescent CDs from Syzygium cumini for metalion sensing and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Qurtulen,
Ahmad, Anees
Salimi Shahraki, Hesam
Khan, Nazoora
Ahmad, Mehraj
Bushra, Rani
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon dots (CDs) derived fromSyzygium cumini leaves were synthesized through a hydrothermal process and employed as a sensing probe for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+. The synthesized CDs underwent comprehensive analysis to discern their functional groups, composition, morphology, nature, and size. The CDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission fluorescence with a quantum yield of 15.9 %. Notably, Fe3+ induced a substantial quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs compared to other metal ions. The developed fluorescent probe facilitated the selective detection of Fe3+ within a linear range of 0–300 µM, exhibiting a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.13 µM. Moreover, the applicability of the fluorescent probe was validated in real water samples, with the recovery and Relative Standard Deviation (RSD) values serving as indicators of specificity and selectivity. Additionally, the CDs were assessed for cell imaging in both normal human and cancer cells. The findings indicate that the produced CDs exhibit minimal cytotoxicity and outstanding biocompatibility, underscoring their potential for bioimaging applications.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesized fluorescent CDs from Syzygium cumini for metalion sensing and cell imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon dots (CDs) derived fromSyzygium cumini leaves were synthesized through a hydrothermal process and employed as a sensing probe for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+. The synthesized CDs underwent comprehensive analysis to discern their functional groups, composition, morphology, nature, and size. The CDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission fluorescence with a quantum yield of 15.9 %. Notably, Fe3+ induced a substantial quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs compared to other metal ions. The developed fluorescent probe facilitated the selective detection of Fe3+ within a linear range of 0–300 µM, exhibiting a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.13 µM. Moreover, the applicability of the fluorescent probe was validated in real water samples, with the recovery and Relative Standard Deviation (RSD) values serving as indicators of specificity and selectivity. Additionally, the CDs were assessed for cell imaging in both normal human and cancer cells. The findings indicate that the produced CDs exhibit minimal cytotoxicity and outstanding biocompatibility, underscoring their potential for bioimaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: A captivating member of the carbon family is carbon dots (CDs), primarily composed of carbon with a size less than 10 nm and featuring an amorphous or crystalline core [1,2]. CDs are highly fluorescent nanomaterials, owing to their significant quantum confinement and possessing highly tunable photoluminescent and optoelectronic properties [3].

CDs gained immense attention because of their unique physical and chemical properties, including photoluminescence stability, chemically inertness, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [4]. Moreover, CDs have electron transfer and reservoir properties, making them versatile for a range of applications, such as photocatalysis, bioimaging, medical diagnosis, biosensors, nanomedicine, and energy sources [5]. CDs exhibit high potential for wastewater treatment owing to their high stability, increased water dispersibility, low toxicity, cost-effective fabrication, and exceptional photostability [6].

For the production of CDs, biomass waste is considered an excellent and sustainable carbon source. Biomass-derived CDs, containing carbonaceous and heteroatom molecules, are not susceptible to surface passivation, unlike CDs produced synthetically. Among all reported methods, the hydrothermal approach is generally preferred for synthesizing CDs from low-cost sources. Recently, CDs have been successfully fabricated from various natural carbon sources, such as citric acid [7], papaya [8], grass [6], tamarind leaves [9], sugarcane baggase [10], humic acid [11], denatured milk [12], coconut husk [13], and waste material, including kitchen waste [14]. Akansha et al. synthesized and characterized monodispersed CDs from fennel seeds with a quantum yield of 9.5 % using a hydrothermal process [15]. Similarly, other reported studies demonstrated the use of cabbage and sugarcane bagasse pulp as carbon sources for synthesizing the fluorescent CDs with quantum yields of 16.5 % and 18.7 %, respectively [4,10].

Iron is among the essential trace elements found in the human body. Fe3+ ions play a crucial role in various metabolic processes, such as hemoglobin formation, DNA synthesis, enzyme catalysis, and electron transport, etc [16]. However, abnormal levels of Fe3+ ions can disrupt the proper functioning of the organism, potentially leading to disorders such as anemia, hemochromatosis, hepatitis, arthritis, diabetes, heart failure, and cancer [17]. Detecting and diagnosing these disorders necessitate the development of an accurate, simple, and sensitive method for Fe3+ ion detection. Various techniques for detecting and quantifying Fe3+ ions have been developed [18–19]. Despite the remarkable accuracy of these technologies, their widespread use is constrained since they are expensive, complex instrumentation requirements, and time-consuming pretreatment, limiting their applications. Fluorescence-based sensing offers has significant advantages such as high selectivity, sensitivity, specificity, and rapid response, making it applicable in biomedical and environmental monitoring [17]. Fluorescence analysis holds high potential for developing and detecting various water pollutants in ecological samples [21]. Quantum dots-based fluorescence sensors, with their exceptional fluorescence characteristics, convenient monitoring, excellent selectivity, and quick response, currently provide a viable alternative for Fe3+ ion detection. Although semiconductor quantum dots are the main research focus on fluorescence probes, the presence of heavy metals within them poses potential risks to both human health and the environment [22,23]. However, concerns about cytotoxicity and photobleaching susceptibility of semiconductor quantum dots limit their further applications [24,25]. It is imperative to design metal-free sensors for Fe3+ detection that are environmentally friendly and exhibit excellent sensitivity. CDs have garnered significant attention because of their exceptional characteristics, and are considered promising fluorescent probes[26–27]. Numerous biomass-based CDs with extremely high sensitivity and selectivity have been reported as effective Fe3+ sensors [29–30]. Syzygium cumini (black plum), a native Indian fruit, offers various therapeutic and physiological advantages, including diabetes prevention, digestive aid, skin health improvement, increased hemoglobin, promotes a healthy heart, maintains oral health, and is suitable for the liver. Beyond being a fruit, its bark, leaves, and seeds are brimming with therapeutic qualities, extensively used in traditional medicine. In traditional Chinese medicine, dried black plum serves a multitude of roles, encompassing the alleviation of liver, spleen, and digestive maladies. Moreover, its reputed astringent attributes are thought to offer respite from issues such as profuse sweating and heightened thirst, along wide associated symptoms. Black plum contains numerous phytochemicals, including alkaloids, flavonoids, steroids, phenols, and tannins, etc [32]. Specifically, the novel process of creating CDs from Ayurvedic plant leaves represents a burgeoning breakthrough with immense potential in the realm of biomedical diagnosis. The careful selection of precursors for synthesizing CDs holds notable significance in the context of the Indian Ayurvedic system of disease treatment, offering a highly economical and readily accessible solution throughout the country. CDs could be one of the most promising ways to boost the utilization potential of the leaves. However, as far as we are concerned, there are no reports of CDs synthesis from black plum leaves.

In this study, we propose a simple, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly one-pot approach for synthesizing CDs derived from black plum leaves. Various analytical techniques were employed to investigate the structural characteristics and surface functionalities of the CDs. Subsequently, we assessed the efficacy of the CDs as afluorescence sensor for detecting Fe3+ ions, achieving a calculated limit of detection of 0.13 µM. Moreover, we evaluated the sensitivity and selectivity of the CDs probe by detecting other metal cations in environmental samples, manifesting their potential for practical applications. Finally, we utilized the MTT [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide] assay to determine the cytotoxicity of the CDs using MCF-7 as a model cell for bioimaging applications.

The leaves of black plum were collected from the campus of Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh, India. Calcium chloride CaCl2, Ferric chloride (FeCl3), Zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2), Nickel chloride (NiCl2), Potassium hydroxide (KOH), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Chromium chloride (CrCl3), Cadmium chloride (CdCl2), Lead chloride (PbCl2), Manganese chloride (MnCl2), Copper chloride (CuCl2), Magnesium chloride (MgCl2), Sodium hydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) and Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4). All chemicals were purchased from CDH and Merck, India. The chemicals employed in this study were of analytical grade, and no further purification procedures were applied. Deionized water was exclusively utilized in all experimental procedures.

CDs were synthesized from black plum leaves using a one pot hydrothermal process (Fig. 1 ). In brief, leaves were taken, washed with distilled water, and sun-dried for one day to remove moisture. The dried leaves were then crushed and transformed into a powdered form. Subsequently, 5 gm of the powdered leaves were added to 50 mL of double distilled water and stirred for 1 h. Following that, the mixture was transferred into the Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and maintained at 180° C for 2 h. The resulting suspension was centrifuged for 30 min at 1500 rpm and then washed with distilled water multiple times to obtain the desired CDs. Finally, the CDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use [33].

The morphological structure was analyzed by high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, TEM2100, JEOL, Japan). The functional groups and chemical composition were conducted through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Model Spectum-BX, USA), and X-ray photoelectron emission spectroscopy (XPS), performed by Thermofisher Scientific, USA utilizing a 300 W Al Kα monochromatic source, involving the preparation of samples to eliminate impurities. Absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, America) and Jasco FP-6300 spectrofluorometer with excitation slit set at 10 nm band pass and emission at 10 nm band pass in 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cell. Time resolved flourescence dacay test were conducted with an FLS-1000 spectroflorometer (EDINBURGH INSTRUMENTS). In the context of Fluorescence Lifetime Spectroscopy (FLS), the process includes dissolving samples in appropriate solvents. Once the ideal excitation and emission wavelengths are identified, the sample is excited with short pulses of light. By gauging the temporal gap between excitation and emission, valuable insights regarding molecular dynamics, interactions, fluorophores environment are obtained. Confocal images were captured by employing the Laser Confocal Scanning Microscope (LCMS, LSM 800, Carl Zeiss, Baden-Württemberg, Germany).

The quantum yield (QY) of photoluminescent (PL) emission from the synthesized CDs was determined using the established method [4]. Qunine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was used as reference (Q: 54 % at 340 nm). Q was evaluated using the following equation:

Where Q is the quantum yield, I and A represent the emission intensity and absorbance of the sample, respectively, and η denotes the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript R refers to the standard.

The as-synthesized CDs were adjusted to the concentration of 500 µg mL−1 in solutions containing different metal ions (Fe3+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Cr3+). After allowing the reaction to proceed for 10 min, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. The sensitivity and selectivity were measured in triplicate. To determine the detection range of Fe3+, 1 mL of an aqueous solution of CDs (100 µg mL−1) was added to 1000 µL of saline phosphate buffer (PBS, pH = 7.4). Subsequently, varying concentrations of Fe3+ (standard solution) were added. The fluorescence intensity at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm was recorded after 10 min [34].

The LOD was calculated by using following formula:

The detection of Fe3+ in real water samples was performed to illustrate the practical application of the developed CDs fluorescent probe. Water samples from various sources, such as rivers and ponds, were collected from Aligarh, India, and tap water was obtained from our laboratory. Prior to detection, the water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The environmental samples were spiked with standard iron ion solutions (2 µM to 50 µM) and subsequently diluted with 2 mL of PBS containing CDs. The solutions were then analyzed using the aforementioned method.

Cell viability was assessed using the MTT assay [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] at a concentration of 5 mg mL−1. This assay exploits the ability of live cells with functional mitochondria to transform tetrazolium salt into formazan crystals [35]. In brief, the growing human breast cells and cancer cells (MFC-7) were seeded in 96-well flat-bottom plate (1 × 104 cells /well) and incubated with varying concentrations of synthesized CDs at 37˚C for 24 h in a humidified chamber with 5 % CO2. Untreated cells served as controls. In each well, 20 μL of 5 mg mL−1 MTT solution was added and incubated for an additional 4 h at 37˚C. Subsequently, cells were washed three times with saline phosphate buffer and dried in air at room temperature. The precipitated formazan was dissolved in 100 μL DMSO, and absorbance was recorded using an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) reader (Thermo Molecular Devices Co, Union City, NJ, USA) at 540 nm. Cell viability was expressed as the percentage of absorbance in CDs treated cells compared to untreated cells and calculated using the following equation:

Where, OD control represents the optical density acquired in the absence of CDs, and OD treated was achieved in the presence of CDs. The experiment was repeated three times.

The surface characteristics were examined using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The TEM image at higher magnification (depicted in Fig. 2 (A)) revealed the spherical configuration and amorphous composition of the CDs. Moreover, the HRTEM image (Fig. 2(B)) displayed a distinct fringe separation of 0.34 nm, aligning with the (002) interlayer gap of graphite [36]. The average size distribution, as determined using Image-J software, ranged between 2 and 6 nm, with an average diameter of 3.4 nm, suggesting a narrow size distribution of CDs (Fig. 2. (C)). For the determination of functional groups, the FTIR spectrum was recorded (Fig. 2 (D)). The broad peak at 3437 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The peaks observed at 2100 and 1642 cm−1 are ascribed to the vibrations of alkyne (CC) and CO, respectively[37]. Additionally, the peak at 697 cm−1 denoted the stretching frequency of the epoxy C-O bond [38]. The XPS analysis comprehensively examined the functional groups and elemental composition of the synthesized CDs. In Fig. 3 (A), three discernible peaks at 285 eV, 400 eV, and 583 eV are evident for C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The synthesized product predominantly consisted of C (78.72 %), N (2.71 %), and O (18.57 %). The high-resolution spectrum of C1s as illustrated in Fig. 3(B), revealed four prominent peaks. (Fig. 3(B)). The peak at 289 eV was attributed to the CC bond, while peaks at 285, 286.5, and 287.1 eV corresponded to the CN, COH, and CO bonds, respectively. Additionally, two smaller peaks at 294 and 298 eV in the C1s high-resolution spectrum were associated with CC and CO bonds, respectively [39,40]. In the high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 3(C)), peaks at 532 eV and 533.8 eV indicated the presence of COH and CO groups. [41,42]. Notably, deconvoluted N1s spectrum displayed three peaks at about 400, 402.6, and 396 eV respectively. The first peak represented the bonding structure of N (C)3, while the latter two reflected the bonding structures of NH and CNC [43,44] (Fig. 3(D)). The N1s peak at 396 eV in the XPS spectrum is indicative of nitrogen-containing functional groups within CDs. Further elaborating on the significance of the prominent 396 eV peak in the N1s XPS spectrum, this specific binding energy denotes the presence of nitrogen atoms within the CD structure. Such nitrogen-containing functional groups, exemplified by amino (–NH2) or amide (–NH-) groups, play a pivotal role in shaping the surface chemistry of CDs. The presence of these nitrogen functionalities significantly influences the unique properties and reactivity of CDs, making them applicable across diverse scientific fields. FTIR and XPS spectra collectively revealed the existence of functional groups with oxygen and nitrogen elements, contributing to the excellent water solubility of this intriguing fluorescent material.

The Raman spectra of the CDs (Figure S1) exhibits two broad peaks at approximately 1590 and 1370 cm−1, potentially attributed to the G (sp3 carbon) and D (sp2 carbon) bands, respectively. The amorphous character of synthesized CDs is highlighted by the inclusion of additional bands. The D band corresponds to glassy carbon or graphite, representing the vibrations of the carbon atoms with tumbling bonds in the termination plane. The G band is consistent with the pulsation of sp2-carbon in the hexagonal lattice of 2D carbon and is comparable to the mode E2g of graphite [45].

The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (Fig. 4 (A)) revealed a prominent peak at 275 nm, corresponding to the n-π* transition of the CO bond [30]. The emission spectra of CDs exhibited a strong emission intensity peak at 460 nm under an excitation wavelength of 330 nm, indicative of the recombination of photogenerated excitons [46]. The QY was determined to be 15.9 %, underscoring the exceptional fluorescence properties of CDs. Moreover, as the excitation wavelength increases from 290 to 450 nm, the fluorescent peaks exhibited a red-shift (Fig. 4(B)). The strongest emission was obtained at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm, establishing the excitation-dependent emissive nature of CDs. This phenomenon is attributed to distinct electronic states induced by functional groups on the surface of CDs [47]. Fig. 4(C) displays images of CDs under daylight and UV-light, showcasing intriguing characteristics under different conditions. In natural light, CDs demonstrate distinctive fluorescence properties, rendering them valuable for a range of applications, including bioimaging and sensor technology. Under UV light, CDs exhibit the ability to emit light of varying colors, underscoring their adaptability in response to diverse stimuli. Furthermore, the interaction between CDs and metal ions introduces fascinating alterations in their optical and chemical attributes, unlocking potential avenues for the sensing and detecting specific ions (Fig. 7 (E)).

Time-resolved fluorescence lifetime spectra were recorded for CDs to explore their quenching behavior. The decay curve obtained after excitation at 360 nm, was optimally fitted using a tri-exponential model:

Where τ1, τ2 and τ3 are different decay time and A1, A2 and A3 are corresponding amplitude [48,49].

Fig. 5 (A) illustrates the decay curve of CDs prior to and subsequent to the introduction of a metal ion. The parameters of fitting and calculated values are presented in Table 1 . The computed average lifespan (τavg) of the synthesized CDs is 5.2 ns. Upon the addition of [Fe3+ (0.02 µM)], this average lifespan decreases to 5.13 ns. The reduction in decay time may be attributed to radiative depopulation caused by band edge recombination [29,44]. The introduction of Fe3+ did not lead to a significant change in the lifespan, supporting the notion of a static quenching mechanism involving the formation of a complex between the fluorophore and Fe3+ in their ground state, altering the local environment and protecting the fluorophore from quenching. Alternatively, other factors such as ligand interactions or energy transfer mechanisms may also play a role [48].
The average lifetime was calculated by the following equation:

Fig. 5 ((B)& (C)) illustrates a discernible reduction in UV absorbance and fluorescence intensity of CDs upon the introduction of Fe3+ ions, attributable to a convergence of surface modifications and quenching effects. Fe3+ ions, renowned for their efficacy as fluorescence quenchers, act to deactivate the excited state of CDs through non-radiative pathways. The CDs excitation energy undergoes interception by Fe3+ ions via static interactions or dynamic collisional processes, resulting in diminished fluorescence emission. Simultaneously, the incorporation of Fe3+ ions induces alterations in the surface chemistry of the CDs. These changes may stem from the coordination of Fe3+ with functional groups (oxygen atom in COOH, –OH, and nitrogen atom in –NH2) on the CDs surface, thereby instigating modifications in the electronic structure. Such interactions give rise to the formation of complexes or aggregates that impact the intrinsic optical properties of the CDs, manifesting as a decrease in both UV absorption and fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, the interaction between Fe3+ ions and specific functional groups on the CDs surface facilitates energy transfer mechanisms antagonistic to fluorescence emission. This process directs the absorbed energy towards non-radiative channels, contributing to the observed decline in fluorescence intensity. The optical behavior of the CDs is intricately influenced by the interplay between surface alterations induced by the presence of Fe3+ ions and quenching phenomena. This complex interaction underscores the nuanced modulation of the CDs optical properties in response to Fe3+ ions [49]. Changes in the chemical environment and surface modifications are the main causes of the shift of peaks to higher values of CDs in the Raman spectrum that occurs after the addition of Fe3+ ions (as shown in Fig. 5 (D)). The overall energy states of the CDs may be affected by the coordination of Fe3+ with surface groups, which might lead to the creation of surface-bound species or charge transfer complexes [50]. Consequently, depending on the particulars of the interactions, the CDs absorption or emission peaks may undergo a redshift or a blueshift. A move to higher values indicates that modifications brought about by the addition of Fe3+ have resulted in the need for additional energy for electronic transitions[51].

In addition, the zeta potential value of CDs was determined to be −14.3 mV, as shown in Fig. 5((E). This observation confirms the existence of negatively charged groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl) on the surface of CDs. Based on this result, it can be inferred that negatively charged CDs, in conjuction with these functional groups, establish an excellent platform for the binding of Fe3+ ions. Upon the introduction of Fe3+, a noticeable reduction in the zeta potential of CDs occured, reaching −8.4 mV, as illustrated in Fig. 5(F). This phenomenon can be attributed to the adsorption of Fe3+ onto the surface of CDs, coinciding with a concomitant decrease in fluorescence intensity, indicative of quenching. The observed decline in zeta potential, from −14.3 to −8.4 mV subsequent to the introduction of Fe3+ ions, can be attributed to a multitude of surface interactions. Notably, the adsorption of Fe3+ ions onto the CDs surface emerges as a plausible cause. The negative charge on the CDs experienced partial neutralization with the addition of positively charged Fe3+ ions, consequently diminishing the magnitude of the zeta potential [52]. Additionally, the distribution of surface charges underwent alterations due to the formation of surface complexes between the CDs and Fe3+ ions. This interaction and potential complexation exerted discernible effects on the electrostatic characteristics, influencing the zeta potential. Moreover, considerations of aggregation or agglomeration were taken into account, contributing to alterations in the total surface charge and diminishing interparticle repulsion. Collectively, these events underscore the intricate interplay between CDs and Fe3+ ions within the colloidal system, substantiating the observed fluctuations in zeta potential.

Fig. 6
illustrates the effect of various parameters (ionic strength, pH, light, and preservation time) on the stability of CDs. The ionic strength tolerance of CDs was assessed by monitoring the variation in the PL intensity of CDs with increasing concentrations of NaCl. In Fig. 6(A), it is observed that increasing the NaCl concentration to 1 M has a minimal effect on PL intensity, suggesting the resilience of CDs to high salt concentrations. The pH-dependent behavior of CDs is examined in Fig. 6(B). The normalized PL intensity of CDs remains relatively stable within the pH range of 1 to 8. However, there is a noticeable decrease in intensity is observed between pH values of 8 and 12, indicating effective usability of CDs under normal physiological pH conditions. In Fig. 6(C), the PL intensity of CDs shows a slight decrease after continuous exposure to a 150 W Xe lamp for 60 min. Additionally, it is observed that the PL intensity of CDs remain above 90 % after 30 days of exposure to natural light at room temperature (Fig. 6(D)), affirming the photostability of CDs. These observations underscore the suitability of the synthesized CDs for physiologically relevant sensing applications [53]. Ensuring the stability of CDs in each batch is crucial to maintain their fluorescence properties and detection performance. To achieve this, several strategies and quality control measured during the synthesis and storage of CDs [54]. The following are are some key considerations:
1.
Synthesis Protocol Optimization: We rigorously control and standardize synthesis parameters such as reaction time, temperature, precursor concentrations, and pH to replicate optimal conditions consistently.
2.
Maintain purity of the precursor: High-quality carbon precursors are carefully sourced to maintain purity and consistency, minimizing variations in starting materials across batches.
3.
Standardized Synthesis Protocol: A detailed and standardized synthesis protocol is strictly adhered to for each batch, outlining specific reagent quantities, reaction steps, and durations.
4.
Characterization Techniques: We use robust characterization techniques, including transmission electron microscopy, and UV–Vis spectroscopy, to assess size distribution, morphology, and optical properties.
5.
Storage Conditions: Optimal storage conditions, controlling temperature, humidity, and light exposure, are maintained to prevent degradation.
6.
Long-Term Stability Studies: Long-term stability studies involve periodic characterization of stored CDs, enabling monitoring of changes in fluorescence intensity and other relevant parameters.

Optimizing various parameters is imperative to enhance the efficacy of the detection process using a fluorescence probe. Key factors such as pH and reaction time play pivotal roles in achieving optimal performance for fluorescence-based assays. Rigorous optimization procedures are indispensable to ensure the accuracy and reliability of data obtained through these assays. In this study, the detection process of the fluorescence probe underwent systematic optimization across a range of pH values (2–11) and reaction times (5–30 min), as depicted in Figure S2. Notably, the most significant enhancement in detection performance was observed at pH 7 with a reaction time of 10 min, indicating the synthesized fluorescence probe's heightened efficiency under these optimized conditions.

The fluorescence quenching effect of different metal ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Co3+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Cr2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ni2+; concentration-300 µM) on CDs (10 mg L-1) were investigated. While most metal ions showed minimal or no change in intensity, Fe3+ ions specifically quenched the PL intensity of CDs (Fig. 7(A)). The observed results underscored the strong quenching effect of Fe3+ on CDs compared all other metal cations, suggesting the potential use of CDs as a selective indicator sensor for Fe3+ ions. The sensing mechanism was attributed to the fluorescence quenching effect initiated through chelation or coordination interactions between the Fe3+ ions and the functional groups on the surface of CDs (–COOH, –OH, C-N, etc.). The adsorbed Fe3+ ions on the CDs surface could readily coordinate with the OH group on the edge of the CDs, resulting in an electron transfer from CDs (excited state) to the vacant 3d orbital of the Fe3+ ion. This process resulted in the dissociation of charge carriers and subsequent fluorescence quenching [55]. The possible quenching mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 7(B). Fig. 7(C) demonstrates the fluorescence quenching of CDs at different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. As the concentration of Fe3+ ions increased, a gradual decrease in the CDs intensity was observed, indicating effective fluorescence quenching of CDs upon the addition of Fe3+, highlighting the high sensitivity of the sensing system to Fe3+ ion concentration. The coordination between Fe3+ and the carboxyl group of CDs increased with the increase in the concentration of Fe3+ ions, resulting in enhanced non-radiative recombination of the charge carriers and consequent quenching. Moreover, no considerable interference was observed at higher concentrations of Fe3+ ions (300 µM). Given the essential role of iron in human metabolism and various biochemical processes, the visible fluorescent method for Fe3+ detection holds significant importance. Fig. 7(D) presents the Stern-Volmer plot of CDs, demonstrating a strong linear correlation between fluorescence intensity (Fo/F) and the increasing concentration of Fe3+ ions (0.02 to 80 µM, R2 = 0.993). Nonlinear fitting of the plot across the entire concentration range (0.02 to 300 µM) suggested the presence of both quenching (static and dynamic) in the system [56]. The quenching efficiency was calculated using the following equation:
(5)
F

F
= 1 +
K
sv
[ Q ]

Where Fo and F denote the fluorescence intensity of CDs at excitation and emission wavelengths of 330 & 420 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively, Ksv and Q represent the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and the concentration of Fe3+. The LOD value was determined as 0.13 μ M, where S/N = 3, with S representing the standard deviation and N denoting the slope. Notably, this LOD value surpassed those reported in existing literature. The CDs derived from black plum were demonstrated to be an innovative and effective signal fluorescent probe for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ across various concentration ranges, exhibiting an exceptionally low detection limit.. The comprehensive findings are summarized in Table 2 , highlighting the uniqueness of our research compared to current literature. This tabular presentation systematically outlines key aspects of our work, emphasizing the distinctive contributions and advancements we introduce to the field. Through visual representation, we aim to facilitate reader comprehension and underscore the exceptional value of our research within the current scholarly landscape.

Fig. 7 (E) depicts the visual images of various metal ions (Fe3+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Cr3+ and Al3+ at a concentration of 300 µM) with CDs dispersion under 330 nm. The image illustrates a remarkable fluorescence quenching (the color of solution changes from dark green to light green fluorescence) for only Fe3+ compared to the other metal ions. This observation suggests that the fluorescent CDs synthesized in our study may serve as a selective indicator sensor for Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in the environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The environmental applicability of CDs was demonstrated by analyzing the Fe3+ in real water samples. Standard addition experiments were performed with different water samples to validate this method. All the samples were filtered and centrifuged to remove suspended particles. A decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CDs was observed when the samples were spiked with standard solutions. Fluorescence responses were recorded with each addition of Fe3+ at varying concentrations. The following equation determined the recoveries of all the samples:
(6)
R e c o v e r y =
C 2
-
C 1
C 0
× 100

Where C0 represents the added concentration of Fe3+ in the environmental samples, and C1 and C2 denote the Fe3+ concentrations in the samples in the absence and presence of a standard Fe3+ solution, respectively. The recovery values for all the samples fall within the range of 88–106.5 %, and the value for RSD (relative standard deviation) range from 0.649 to 1.272 %. These results demonstrate the high accuracy of the proposed method, as summarized in Table 3 .

The potential application of synthesized CDs in normal human breast and cancer cells was explored using an MTT assay. Within this analytical framework, functional cellular enzymes within the cells transformed MTT into an insoluble blue-violet formazan crystal. Cells were incubated to varying concentrations of CDs (0 to 1 mg mL−1) for 24 h. Results obtained from the assay revealed that the addition of a control did not any impact on cell viability. Conversely, breast cells treated with CDs exhibited a mere 4.71 % reduction in cell viability (Fig. 8 (A)), with a survival rate exceeding 94 %, even at higher CDs concentrations. In parallel, examination of cancer cells (MFC-7 cell line), demonstrated a pronounced inhibitory effect on cell viability upon treatment with CDs, as illustrated in Fig. 8(B). A discernible inverse correlation between the survival rate of cells and CDs concentration emerged, suggesting a doze-dependent inhibitory effect. The findings of this study collectively propose a relatively low cytotoxicity of synthesized CDs towards both normal and cancer cells, thus positioning CDs as promising candidates for prospective biomedical applications.

Moreover, the cellular uptake of CDs was investigated using Laser Scanning Confocal Microscopy. In Fig. 8(C) the incubation of CDs at a concentration of 32 µg mL−1 with MFC-7 cells over a 24-h period is depicted under bright field illumination. Fig. 8 ((D)&(E)) present the green fluorescence observed upon 540 nm laser excitation and merged images (combining fluorescence and bright field), respectively. These findings illustrate the capability of CDs to traverse the cellular membrane via endocytosis. The bright signal signifies the successful utilization of CDs for labelling cancer cells, thereby facilitating the visualization of breast cancer cells in both in vitro and in vivo imaging[57]. Consequently, CDs emerge as an exemplary choice for deployment as a fluorescent bioimaging agent, given their low cytotoxicity and exceptional biocompatibility.

In this study, we present the hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent and cost-effective CDs derived from black plum leaves. The resulting CDs exhibit a notable quanutm yield of 15.9 %. The characterization of the CDs was conducted using FTIR and XPS to identify carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups. HRTEM analysis determined the size distribution of the CDs within the range of 2 to 6 nm. The CDs were demonstrated to function as efficient fluorescent sensor for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions, achieving a detection limit of 0.13 μ M. Moreover, the practical applicability of the CDs was showcased by successfully detecting Fe3+ ions in environmental water samples. The robust selectivity and sensitivity of the CDs for Fe3+ in environmental samples were validated through recovery tests, yielding values ranging from 86.6 % to 106.1 %, with RSDs of less than 1.1 %. Additionally, the PL intensity of the CDs remained unchanged after irradiation with a Xe lamp (500 W) for 60 min and storage for one month, indicating their exceptional stability. A cell cytotoxicity assay demonstrated that the developed CDs are less toxic to both normal and cancer cells. The versatile nature of the CDs was further exemplified by their application as a sensing probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions and in cell imaging, highlighting their potential for diverse scientific and technological applications.
